The trailing P/E of 9.6× sits just inside the Energy sector benchmark range of 8–14×, and EV/EBITDA of 5.4× is below the sector median peer of 5.6×; on forward P/E ONGC is the cheapest in its peer group at 6.8× (P0 rank), and P/B of 1.0× is 39.8% below the sector median — collectively grading as cheap, though the discount reflects genuine earnings headwinds rather than pure mispricing. The biggest risk is that elevated crude at $105.42 does not fully flow through to ONGC's realisations due to government-mandated domestic gas pricing caps and potential subsidy burden revival, while a sharply weakening INR (95.96) raises import-linked costs and fiscal pressure that could prompt the government to extract higher dividends or impose fresh levies on ONGC's upstream profits.

At a single-quarter annualised EPS run-rate of roughly ₹70, and given that two-wheeler OEMs typically trade at 30–45x earnings in India, the stock is unlikely to be cheap at current market levels, though the expanding EV portfolio (King EV Max in Nepal) signals a credible medium-term growth pivot.

Varun Beverages (VBL) is India's largest PepsiCo franchisee bottler, with a rapidly expanding international footprint (including the newly acquired Twizza in South Africa), operating in a structurally growing Indian beverage market underpinned by favourable demographics and premiumisation. In Q1 CY2026, revenue grew +18.1% YoY to ₹6,574 cr and PAT grew +20.1% YoY to ₹879 cr, with volume growth of 16.3% (India +14.4%, International +21.4%) and EBITDA margin expanding 55 bps to 23.3% — a strong and consistent operational trajectory, though only one quarter of trend data is available in this dataset, limiting trajectory confidence. At a trailing P/E of 57.5× (vs FMCG sector median ~50× and benchmark range of 40–60×), VBL sits at the upper end of fair but is ranked P83 among FMCG peers on trailing P/E, with forward P/E of 44.6× confirming earnings growth is priced in — grading this as expensive relative to peers even if not egregiously so in absolute FMCG terms, especially given the stock trading at 98.8% of its 52-week range. The biggest risk is the combination of high financial leverage (D/E of 12.71×), rising finance costs from Twizza acquisition funding, declining realization per case in India (-1.5% due to pack upsizing), and a stagflationary macro backdrop (rising crude at $105/bbl, INR at 95.96) which pressures PET/sugar input costs and squeezes real consumer purchasing power.

Bank of Baroda is a large public-sector bank (PSB) with a global business book of ₹30.78 lakh crore, reporting its highest-ever quarterly and annual PAT — Q4FY26 net profit of ₹5,616 cr (+11.2% YoY) and FY26 full-year PAT of ₹20,021 cr (+11.2% YoY), with loan growth accelerating to 16.2% YoY (best in 10 quarters), a healthy NIM of 2.89%, Gross NPA at a low 1.89%, and PCR at a very strong 93.94%, all pointing to a bank in sound and improving fundamental shape. Valuation is unambiguously cheap: trailing P/E of 6.92× sits well below the PSB sector benchmark range of 12–18× and is ranked P0 (cheapest) among 16 Financials peers, while P/B of 0.83× against an ROE of 12.7% further confirms significant undervaluation — for context, P/B < 1.5 with ROE > 12% is a textbook cheap signal for banks. Management has raised loan growth guidance to 12–14% and set a full-year credit cost target below 0.60%, with ROA above 1% — a confident forward tone. The biggest risk is that PSB re-rating has historically been slow due to governance concerns, state ownership overhang, and the macro headwind of a weakening INR (95.96) and rising crude ($105.42) which could pressure credit quality in rate-sensitive segments and squeeze NIMs if deposit costs prove stickier than guided.

Trailing P/E of 50.25× sits well above the Industrials sector median of 28–45×, making BEL expensive on that metric; however, forward P/E compresses to 36.67× (a 27% discount to trailing), implying strong earnings growth is already expected by the market, and EV/EBITDA of 36.8× is roughly at the peer median (P60), suggesting fair-to-moderately-rich relative pricing rather than extreme overvaluation. The single biggest risk is that BEL trades at a significant premium to sector benchmarks and any slowdown in defence budget allocations, execution delays, or the six Independent Director vacancies crystallising into a governance overhang could trigger a sharp de-rating.

Samvardhana Motherson International is a global auto-components supplier with diversified OEM customers across wiring harnesses, mirrors, and polymer components, making it one of the largest auto ancillaries listed in India. The yfinance quarterly data shows sequential revenue improvement from ₹16,142 cr (Q1FY26) to ₹16,769 cr (Q3FY26), with PAT recovering sharply from ₹512 cr to ₹1,024 cr over the same three quarters — a positive intra-year trajectory, though the multi-quarter BSE-sourced trend table is empty and full-year FY26 audited numbers are not yet available for YoY comparison. At a trailing P/E of 37.2× against a sector median of 18–28×, MOTHERSON is materially expensive on earnings multiples (P83 in its peer set), though EV/EBITDA of 13.2× is actually a 16% discount to the sector median of 15.7×, reflecting the capital-intensive, debt-heavy nature of the business (D/E of 43.9×) and modest ROE of 10.1% — forward P/E of 20.8× implies the market is pricing in significant earnings recovery ahead. The biggest risk is that elevated crude oil prices and a weakening INR structurally squeeze margins on imported raw materials and energy costs while simultaneously raising interest burden on the high leverage, potentially undermining the earnings recovery thesis underpinning the premium valuation.

fundamental.thesis: IndiGo (InterGlobe Aviation) is India's dominant low-cost carrier with ~60% domestic market share, operating a fleet of 440 aircraft and expanding its international network. On valuation, trailing P/E of 53.5× is well above the Industrials sector median of ~35× and extremely stretched on P/B at 18.3× (P100 among peers), though EV/EBITDA of 16.4× sits at P0 (cheapest among sector peers) and forward P/E of 24× implies meaningful earnings recovery is already priced in by the market. The single biggest risk to the thesis is that the current macro backdrop — rising crude at $105/bbl and a sharply weakening INR at 95.96 — directly attacks IndiGo's two largest cost lines (aviation turbine fuel and USD-denominated lease rentals), threatening the earnings recovery the forward multiple is banking on.

Bosch Limited is India's leading automotive technology and components manufacturer, with dominant positions in fuel injection systems, braking, and increasingly in electrification and 2-wheeler powertrain solutions, backed by its German parent Robert Bosch GmbH. Revenue growth has been broadly stable at around +13% YoY in Q4 FY26, with FY26 full-year revenue up ~11% YoY to ₹200,347 mINR, while PAT growth has been modest at just +2.7% QoQ in Q4 FY26 (PAT margin compressed to ~5% in Q4 vs 13.8% for full-year FY26, the latter inflated by non-recurring asset-sale gains); the 2-Wheeler PowerSports segment is a genuine bright spot at +69% FY-on-FY. Bosch trades at a trailing P/E of 38.3×, which is already above the sector median benchmark for Auto of 18–28×, and is the most expensive stock in its peer group on both forward P/E (36.2×, P100 rank, +74% premium to sector median 20.8×) and EV/EBITDA (37.7×, P100 rank, +107% premium to sector median 18.2×), firmly placing it in expensive territory. The biggest fundamental risk is that the elevated valuation is built on earnings quality that includes non-recurring items (asset disposals) and could face significant derating if auto volumes soften amid high crude prices and INR weakness squeezing margins, particularly in the core diesel/ICE segment which faces structural EV transition headwinds.
